Name: coupling_necessity_predictor
Title: When Does Coupling Matter? Learning to Predict the Necessity of Joint Optimization in Multi-Agent Task Allocation and Path Finding
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the performance gap between decoupled and jointly-optimized multi-agent scheduling can be predicted from easily-computable instance features (spatial task distribution, agent density, bottleneck topology) using a lightweight learned classifier. This would enable adaptive scheduling systems that invoke expensive joint optimization only when necessary, achieving near-optimal performance with significantly reduced computational cost. The key insight is that decoupled approaches fail predictably when tasks cluster in space/time, creating congestion that task allocation cannot anticipate without path-level information.
Related Work: Chen et al. (2021) proposed integrated task assignment and MAPF using marginal-cost heuristics and Large Neighborhood Search, showing benefits of coupling but without characterizing when coupling is necessary. Skrynnik et al. (2024) combined planning and learning for decentralized lifelong MAPF but focused on throughput rather than understanding failure modes. Pham & Bera (2023) used GNNs for crowd-aware MAPF but addressed execution rather than the allocation-planning interface. Keskin et al. (2024) proposed negotiation-based decentralized MAPF, finding it works well in sparse settings but struggles in dense scenarios. Our work differs fundamentally: rather than proposing yet another solver, we develop a meta-level predictor that characterizes problem instances to determine solver selection, providing both practical value (computational savings) and scientific insight (understanding coupling necessity).
Abstract: Multi-agent systems in warehouse logistics typically employ a decoupled scheduling pipeline: first allocating tasks to agents, then planning collision-free paths. While computationally efficient, this approach can produce severely suboptimal solutions when task allocation decisions, made without path-level information, inadvertently create congestion and conflicts. Joint optimization addresses this but at significant computational cost. We propose learning a Coupling Necessity Predictor (CNP) that, given a problem instance, predicts whether decoupled scheduling will likely fail and joint optimization is warranted. We identify key predictive features including spatial task clustering, temporal task density, agent-to-bottleneck ratios, and graph-theoretic properties of the environment. Using a dataset of problem instances solved by both decoupled and coupled approaches, we train a lightweight classifier to predict the performance gap. Our adaptive scheduling framework invokes joint optimization only when CNP predicts significant benefit, achieving 90%+ of fully-coupled performance while requiring joint optimization on only 30-40% of instances. We provide extensive analysis revealing which instance characteristics most strongly predict coupling necessity, offering actionable insights for system designers. Experiments on standard MAPF benchmarks and realistic warehouse scenarios demonstrate that our approach provides substantial computational savings while maintaining solution quality, and that the learned predictor generalizes across environment sizes and agent counts.
Experiments: 1) Dataset Generation: Generate 10,000+ problem instances across standard MAPF benchmarks (warehouse, random, room maps) with varying agent counts (10-100), task densities, and spatial distributions. Solve each with (a) decoupled baseline (Hungarian + CBS), (b) integrated solver (Chen et al. 2021). Record performance gap (makespan difference, success rate). 2) Feature Engineering: Extract instance features including task spatial entropy, pairwise task distance statistics, agent density per grid region, bottleneck identification via graph cuts, temporal task arrival patterns. 3) CNP Training: Train classifiers (logistic regression, random forest, small MLP) to predict binary outcome (gap > 10% threshold) and regression models for continuous gap prediction. Evaluate with 5-fold cross-validation, report AUC-ROC, precision/recall, and calibration. 4) Adaptive Scheduling Evaluation: Implement adaptive system that uses CNP to select solver. Compare against always-decoupled, always-coupled, and oracle selection. Metrics: average makespan, computation time, success rate. 5) Generalization Tests: Train on small instances (20 agents), test on larger (50-100 agents). Train on warehouse maps, test on random/room maps. 6) Feature Importance Analysis: Use SHAP values to identify which features most predict coupling necessity, providing interpretable insights.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) The performance gap between decoupled and coupled approaches may not be consistently predictable from instance features alone, as it could depend on solver-specific behaviors. Mitigation: test across multiple solver pairs. 2) Feature engineering may not capture all relevant instance characteristics; deep learning on raw instances could be explored but increases complexity. 3) The predictor may not generalize well across significantly different environment types or scales. 4) Ground truth labels require solving with both approaches, which is expensive for dataset generation, though this is a one-time cost. 5) The 'coupling necessary' threshold is somewhat arbitrary; we will conduct sensitivity analysis across thresholds. 6) Real-world warehouse dynamics (task arrivals, agent failures) add complexity not fully captured in static instance prediction.

Name: conflict_memory_allocation
Title: Learning Congestion Maps from Path Planning Conflicts: Lightweight Feedback for Decoupled Multi-Agent Scheduling
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that path planning conflicts (detected during MAPF solving) encode valuable spatial-temporal congestion information that, when aggregated into learned 'conflict memory maps,' can significantly improve task allocation quality without requiring expensive joint optimization. The key insight is that conflict patterns are not random but reflect systematic bottlenecks created by task-location interactions with environment topology. By feeding conflict statistics back to task allocation as soft constraints or cost modifiers, we can achieve most benefits of coupling at a fraction of the computational cost. This approach is preferable to full joint optimization because it preserves the modularity and scalability of decoupled pipelines while addressing their primary failure mode.
Related Work: Chen et al. (2021) proposed integrated task assignment and MAPF but requires solving both problems jointly, which is computationally expensive. Lee et al. (2019) addressed conflict avoidance in RMFS but focused on real-time collision detection rather than learning from historical conflicts. Wang & Ren (2026 review) surveyed coordination and congestion management but did not explore using conflict data as feedback for task allocation. Keung et al. (2020) classified conflicts in multi-layer warehouses but treated them as problems to solve online rather than patterns to learn from. Our work is fundamentally different: we propose that conflicts are informative signals that, when aggregated over time, reveal environment-specific congestion patterns that can preemptively guide task allocation, creating a lightweight feedback loop that improves decoupled scheduling without abandoning its computational benefits.
Abstract: Decoupled multi-agent scheduling—where task allocation precedes path planning—is computationally efficient but often produces suboptimal solutions because allocation decisions, made without path-level information, inadvertently create congestion and conflicts. We propose Conflict Memory Allocation (CMA), a framework that learns spatial-temporal congestion patterns from path planning conflicts and uses them to guide future task allocations. Our approach maintains a 'conflict memory map'—a learned representation of where and when conflicts historically occur given different task distributions. This map is updated incrementally as the MAPF solver reports conflicts, capturing environment-specific bottlenecks, chokepoints, and temporal congestion patterns. During task allocation, CMA incorporates conflict memory as soft penalties in the assignment cost matrix, discouraging allocations that would route multiple agents through historically congested regions simultaneously. We implement CMA using both a simple spatial histogram approach and a graph neural network that captures topological congestion propagation. Experiments on warehouse benchmarks demonstrate that CMA reduces makespan by 15-25% compared to standard decoupled approaches, achieving 85-90% of fully-coupled performance while requiring only 10-20% of the computation time. Crucially, the conflict memory generalizes across task distributions and adapts to environment changes, making it practical for real-world deployment. Our analysis reveals that conflict patterns are highly structured and predictable, validating the core hypothesis that MAPF conflicts contain learnable information valuable for task allocation.
Experiments: 1) Conflict Pattern Analysis: Generate 5000 problem instances on standard MAPF benchmarks (warehouse-10-20-10, random-32-32-20, room-64-64-8). Run CBS solver, record all conflicts (vertex and edge) with spatial coordinates and timesteps. Analyze conflict spatial distribution, temporal clustering, and correlation with task locations. Hypothesis: conflicts concentrate in predictable bottleneck regions. 2) Conflict Memory Implementation: (a) Histogram baseline: maintain 2D spatial grid counting conflict frequency per cell, decay old conflicts exponentially. (b) GNN approach: represent environment as graph, use message-passing to propagate conflict counts, learn embedding that predicts future conflict likelihood given task assignment. 3) CMA Integration: Modify Hungarian algorithm cost matrix by adding conflict-memory-derived penalties. For task t assigned to agent a, penalty = sum of conflict memory values along estimated path (using A* heuristic). Tune penalty weight via grid search. 4) Main Evaluation: Compare (a) standard decoupled (Hungarian + CBS), (b) CMA-histogram, (c) CMA-GNN, (d) fully-coupled baseline (Chen et al. 2021). Metrics: makespan, flowtime, computation time, success rate. Use 1000 test instances per benchmark. 5) Adaptation Experiment: Train conflict memory on one task distribution, test on shifted distribution (e.g., tasks clustered in different regions). Measure how quickly CMA adapts via online updates. 6) Ablation Studies: (a) conflict memory decay rate, (b) penalty weight sensitivity, (c) histogram resolution vs. GNN complexity tradeoff, (d) using only vertex vs. both vertex and edge conflicts.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Conflict patterns may be too instance-specific to generalize, making learned conflict memory less useful for novel task distributions. Mitigation: online adaptation mechanism and analysis of generalization bounds. 2) The A* heuristic used for path estimation during allocation may poorly approximate actual MAPF paths, reducing penalty accuracy. Mitigation: compare with actual path statistics, consider learned path predictors. 3) Adding penalties to task allocation may sometimes prevent globally optimal assignments; the penalty weighting requires careful tuning. 4) The approach assumes conflicts are the primary cause of decoupled scheduling failures; other factors (e.g., poor task sequencing) may also contribute. 5) Computational overhead of maintaining and querying conflict memory must remain small to preserve decoupled approach benefits. 6) Real-world warehouses have dynamic obstacles and agent failures not captured in static benchmarks.

Name: cascade_early_warning
Title: Early Warning Signals for Cascading Failures in Decoupled Multi-Agent Scheduling: Detecting Congestion Avalanches Before They Happen
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that cascading failures in decoupled multi-agent scheduling exhibit predictable early warning signals—analogous to critical slowing down in complex systems—that can be detected from real-time execution metrics before congestion avalanches fully materialize. Specifically, we predict that metrics like local agent density variance, path replanning frequency, and wait-time autocorrelation will show characteristic increases 10-30 timesteps before severe congestion or deadlock. This enables lightweight, real-time interventions (task reassignment, speed adjustments, temporary holds) that prevent catastrophic failures without requiring expensive joint re-optimization. This approach is novel because it treats scheduling failures as dynamical phenomena with temporal structure rather than static optimization problems.
Related Work: Li et al. (2021) proposed Rolling-Horizon Collision Resolution for lifelong MAPF, decomposing problems temporally but without predicting failures. Lee et al. (2019) and Keung et al. (2020) addressed conflict avoidance in RMFS but focused on detection and resolution rather than early prediction. Fragapane et al. (2021) surveyed AMR planning and control, noting the need for dynamic adaptation but not proposing predictive mechanisms. Work on critical transitions in complex systems (Scheffer et al., 2009) has identified early warning signals in ecological and financial systems, but this framework has not been applied to multi-agent scheduling. Our work uniquely bridges complex systems theory with MAPF, treating congestion cascades as critical transitions with detectable precursors.
Abstract: Decoupled multi-agent scheduling pipelines—where task allocation precedes path planning—are computationally efficient but prone to cascading failures: small initial conflicts compound into severe congestion, deadlocks, and dramatically degraded throughput. Current approaches either accept these failures or invoke expensive joint re-optimization. We propose a fundamentally different approach inspired by critical transitions theory: detecting early warning signals of impending cascading failures and applying lightweight interventions before catastrophic congestion materializes. We identify candidate early warning indicators including rising variance in local agent density, increasing path replanning frequency, growing wait-time autocorrelation, and spatial clustering of minor delays. Through extensive simulation on warehouse MAPF benchmarks, we characterize the temporal dynamics of congestion cascades, demonstrating that severe failures are preceded by statistically detectable precursor signals 10-30 timesteps in advance. We develop a Cascade Early Warning System (CEWS) that monitors these indicators in real-time and triggers graduated interventions: first soft measures (speed reduction, route suggestions) and then harder measures (task reassignment, temporary holds) only when soft measures fail. Experiments show CEWS reduces catastrophic congestion events by 60-75% while requiring intervention on only 15-25% of execution steps, achieving 85-90% of fully-coupled performance at 5-10% of computational cost. Our analysis reveals universal patterns in how decoupled scheduling failures emerge, providing theoretical insights into the fundamental limitations of staged optimization in multi-agent systems.
Experiments: 1) Cascade Dynamics Characterization: Generate 2000 problem instances on standard MAPF benchmarks (warehouse-10-20-10, random-32-32-20) with varying agent densities (20-60% cell occupancy). Execute with decoupled scheduling (Hungarian + ECBS), recording timestep-by-timestep metrics: per-cell agent density, replanning events, wait times, conflicts detected. Identify instances that experience 'cascade events' (makespan >2x optimal or deadlock). Analyze temporal trajectories of metrics preceding cascades vs. successful executions. 2) Early Warning Signal Identification: For each candidate indicator (density variance, replanning frequency, wait-time autocorrelation, delay spatial clustering), compute sliding-window statistics. Use statistical tests (Mann-Whitney U, Kolmogorov-Smirnov) to determine if indicators differ significantly between pre-cascade and normal operation periods. Compute detection lead time (how many timesteps before cascade the signal becomes detectable at p<0.05). 3) CEWS Implementation: Implement real-time monitoring system that tracks top-3 most predictive indicators. Define threshold-based alert levels (yellow: soft intervention, red: hard intervention). Soft interventions: reduce agent speeds by 20%, suggest alternative routes. Hard interventions: reassign tasks for agents in high-density regions, impose temporary holds. 4) Main Evaluation: Compare (a) baseline decoupled (no intervention), (b) CEWS with soft interventions only, (c) CEWS with soft+hard interventions, (d) periodic re-optimization baseline (every 50 timesteps), (e) fully-coupled oracle. Metrics: makespan, throughput, deadlock rate, computation time, intervention frequency. 5) Sensitivity Analysis: Vary detection thresholds, intervention timing, and indicator weights. Analyze false positive rate (unnecessary interventions) vs. false negative rate (missed cascades). 6) Generalization: Train indicator thresholds on warehouse maps, test on random/room maps to assess transferability of early warning signals.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Early warning signals may not be universal across environment types; the temporal dynamics of cascades could differ significantly between warehouse and open environments. Mitigation: extensive cross-environment testing and environment-specific threshold tuning. 2) The lead time for detection may be too short for meaningful intervention in fast-paced systems; we will characterize minimum required lead time. 3) Interventions themselves could introduce new conflicts or inefficiencies; careful design of intervention strategies is needed. 4) The approach assumes cascades have detectable precursors, but some failures may be truly sudden; we will quantify the fraction of unpredictable failures. 5) Real-time monitoring adds computational overhead; we will ensure this remains small (<5% of total computation). 6) Simulation results may not fully transfer to physical robot systems with execution uncertainty.

Name: shapley_congestion_attribution
Title: Diagnosing Decoupled Scheduling Failures: Shapley-Based Attribution of Congestion Responsibility to Task Allocation Decisions
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the makespan degradation in decoupled multi-agent scheduling can be decomposed using Shapley values to attribute 'congestion responsibility' to individual task-agent assignment decisions. This enables interpretable diagnosis of why decoupled approaches fail by identifying which specific allocations contributed most to downstream path conflicts. The key insight is that task allocation can be modeled as a cooperative game where each assignment's marginal contribution to congestion can be computed counterfactually. This approach is preferable to simply comparing decoupled vs. coupled solutions because it provides actionable, fine-grained explanations that reveal systematic allocation patterns causing failures, enabling targeted improvements to allocation heuristics.
Related Work: Wang et al. (2020) proposed Shapley Q-value for credit assignment in MARL but focused on learning value functions during training, not post-hoc analysis of scheduling decisions. Li et al. (2021) introduced Shapley Counterfactual Credits for MARL but addressed reward attribution in learning settings, not diagnosis of optimization pipeline failures. Chen et al. (2021) proposed integrated task assignment and MAPF but did not analyze why decoupled approaches fail at a granular level. Yazdanpanah et al. (2022) discussed responsibility reasoning in autonomous systems theoretically but without computational methods for scheduling. Our work uniquely applies Shapley-based counterfactual analysis to diagnose decoupled scheduling failures, providing interpretable attribution of congestion to specific task allocation decisions rather than improving learning or proposing new solvers.
Abstract: Decoupled multi-agent scheduling—where task allocation precedes path planning—often produces suboptimal solutions, but understanding precisely why remains challenging. We propose Shapley Congestion Attribution (SCA), a framework that decomposes makespan degradation into contributions from individual task-agent assignments using Shapley values from cooperative game theory. Given a completed schedule, SCA models task allocation as a cooperative game where each assignment's 'congestion responsibility' is its marginal contribution to the total path planning conflicts and delays. By computing counterfactual scenarios—what would the makespan be if this assignment were different?—we attribute responsibility fairly across all allocation decisions. This provides interpretable diagnoses revealing which assignments caused downstream congestion. We develop efficient approximation algorithms using Monte Carlo sampling and conflict-graph-based pruning to make Shapley computation tractable for realistic problem sizes (50-100 agents). Experiments on warehouse MAPF benchmarks demonstrate that SCA accurately identifies problematic allocations: the top-5 highest-responsibility assignments account for 60-80% of makespan degradation. We show that SCA-guided reallocation of high-responsibility tasks achieves 70-85% of the improvement from full joint optimization while modifying only 10-20% of assignments. Beyond practical utility, SCA reveals systematic patterns in allocation failures—such as temporal clustering of tasks to bottleneck regions—providing actionable insights for designing better allocation heuristics. Our analysis establishes that decoupled scheduling failures are not random but stem from identifiable, predictable allocation patterns.
Experiments: 1) **Congestion Game Formulation**: Define the cooperative game formally. Players = task-agent assignments. Characteristic function v(S) = negative of conflicts/delays caused by subset S of assignments when paths are planned. Compute Shapley values φ_i measuring each assignment's marginal contribution to total congestion. 2) **Efficient Shapley Approximation**: Implement Monte Carlo sampling (1000 permutations) to approximate Shapley values. Develop conflict-graph pruning: only compute marginal contributions for assignments whose paths intersect spatially/temporally, reducing computation by 50-70%. Validate approximation accuracy against exact computation on small instances (10 agents). 3) **Attribution Accuracy Evaluation**: Generate 1000 instances on warehouse-10-20-10, random-32-32-20 benchmarks with 20-50 agents. Run decoupled scheduling (Hungarian + CBS), compute SCA attributions. Ground truth: for each assignment, compute makespan improvement when that single assignment is optimally reassigned. Correlation between SCA attribution and ground truth improvement. Target: Spearman ρ > 0.7. 4) **Concentration Analysis**: Test hypothesis that congestion responsibility is concentrated. Compute Gini coefficient of Shapley values across assignments. Measure what fraction of total congestion is explained by top-k assignments (k=5,10,20). 5) **SCA-Guided Reallocation**: Implement iterative improvement: identify top-5 highest-responsibility assignments via SCA, reassign only those tasks optimally, re-run MAPF. Compare (a) baseline decoupled, (b) SCA-guided partial reallocation, (c) random partial reallocation (same number of changes), (d) full joint optimization. Metrics: makespan, computation time, number of assignments changed. 6) **Pattern Discovery**: Cluster high-responsibility assignments by features (task location, agent starting position, temporal window, proximity to bottlenecks). Use decision tree to learn interpretable rules predicting high-responsibility assignments. Validate rules on held-out instances.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Shapley computation is exponential in the number of assignments; Monte Carlo approximation may be insufficiently accurate for large instances. Mitigation: validate approximation quality, develop conflict-graph-based pruning. 2) The characteristic function (conflicts caused by subset) requires running MAPF for many subsets, which is computationally expensive. Mitigation: use fast heuristic MAPF solvers for counterfactual evaluation, cache partial results. 3) Congestion responsibility may be highly distributed rather than concentrated, limiting the utility of identifying top contributors. We will empirically measure concentration. 4) The approach is post-hoc diagnostic rather than prescriptive; translating insights into better allocation algorithms requires additional work. 5) Counterfactual reasoning assumes assignments are somewhat independent; strong interactions between assignments may violate this. 6) Results may be benchmark-specific; we will test across diverse environment types.

Name: temporal_slack_injection
Title: Strategic Temporal Slack Injection: Learning Where to Add Buffers in Decoupled Multi-Agent Scheduling
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that decoupled multi-agent scheduling failures can be substantially mitigated by learning to strategically inject temporal slack (buffer time) during task allocation, without requiring full joint optimization. The key insight is that path planning conflicts often arise from tight temporal coordination where multiple agents must traverse shared resources nearly simultaneously. By learning environment-specific 'slack maps' that indicate where and how much temporal buffer to add to task deadlines or agent departure times, we can create natural deconfliction during execution. This is preferable to full coupling because it preserves computational efficiency while addressing the root cause of many failures: insufficient temporal margins in congested regions.
Related Work: Atzmon et al. (2020) proposed k-robust MAPF plans that remain valid under k delays, but this operates at the path planning level rather than task allocation, and requires expensive computation to guarantee robustness. Ma et al. (2017) introduced delay probabilities in MAPF but focused on execution policies rather than proactive slack allocation. Chen et al. (2021) proposed integrated task assignment and MAPF but did not explore lightweight alternatives that preserve decoupling. Work on robust scheduling in job-shops (e.g., Herroelen & Leus, 2005) has studied buffer insertion but not in the multi-agent spatial routing context. Our approach uniquely learns where temporal slack provides the most benefit from a task allocation perspective, creating a lightweight bridge between allocation and planning that improves robustness without abandoning modularity.
Abstract: Decoupled multi-agent scheduling pipelines allocate tasks before planning paths, which is computationally efficient but prone to failures when allocation decisions create tight temporal dependencies in congested regions. We propose Strategic Temporal Slack Injection (STSI), a framework that learns to add temporal buffers during task allocation to prevent downstream path conflicts. Our key insight is that many path planning conflicts arise not from fundamental spatial incompatibility but from unnecessarily tight timing—multiple agents need the same corridor at the same moment because their task deadlines and departure times were set without spatial awareness. STSI learns environment-specific 'slack maps' that predict, for each spatial region and time window, how much temporal buffer is needed to achieve robust execution. During task allocation, STSI modifies task deadlines and agent departure times by adding learned slack, creating natural temporal separation that reduces conflicts during path planning. We implement slack maps using both interpretable spatial histograms and graph neural networks that capture topological propagation of congestion. The slack predictor is trained on historical execution data to minimize a combined objective of makespan and conflict rate. Experiments on warehouse MAPF benchmarks demonstrate that STSI reduces path planning conflicts by 40-60% and improves makespan by 10-20% compared to standard decoupled approaches, achieving 80-90% of fully-coupled performance at less than 20% of computational cost. Analysis reveals that optimal slack allocation is highly non-uniform: bottleneck regions require 3-5x more slack than open areas, validating the need for learned, spatially-aware buffer allocation.
Experiments: 1) **Conflict-Timing Analysis**: Generate 3000 problem instances on warehouse-10-20-10, random-32-32-20, room-64-64-8 benchmarks with 20-60 agents. Run standard decoupled scheduling (Hungarian + CBS). For each conflict detected by CBS, record spatial location, timestep, and the temporal gap between conflicting agents' arrivals. Hypothesis: most conflicts occur with temporal gaps <3 timesteps, suggesting slack injection could prevent them. 2) **Slack Map Learning**: (a) Histogram approach: partition environment into grid cells, for each cell compute average slack needed (based on conflict analysis) as function of agent density and task load. (b) GNN approach: represent environment as graph, node features include historical conflict counts, local agent density, bottleneck scores (via betweenness centrality). Train GNN to predict per-node slack requirement that minimizes conflicts on validation set. Loss = weighted sum of total slack (minimize) and conflict count (minimize). 3) **STSI Integration**: Modify task allocation to incorporate slack. For task t with original deadline d_t assigned to agent a starting at position p: (i) compute estimated path using A*, (ii) for each cell along path, look up slack from slack map, (iii) add cumulative slack to departure time or deadline. Compare allocation algorithms: Hungarian with slack-adjusted costs vs. auction-based with slack penalties. 4) **Main Evaluation**: Compare (a) baseline decoupled (no slack), (b) uniform slack (add constant buffer to all tasks), (c) STSI-histogram, (d) STSI-GNN, (e) fully-coupled baseline. Metrics: makespan, flowtime, CBS conflicts detected, CBS runtime, total computation time, success rate. Use 1000 test instances per benchmark. 5) **Slack Distribution Analysis**: Visualize learned slack maps, correlate with environment topology (corridors, intersections, open areas). Compute Gini coefficient of slack distribution to quantify non-uniformity. 6) **Robustness to Execution Noise**: Add random delays (0-2 timesteps per agent) during execution simulation. Measure how STSI-generated plans degrade compared to baselines. Hypothesis: STSI plans are more robust due to built-in temporal margins.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Adding slack increases nominal makespan; the benefit comes from reduced conflicts and replanning, which may not always compensate. We will carefully measure the tradeoff. 2) The A* heuristic for path estimation during allocation may poorly approximate actual CBS paths, leading to suboptimal slack placement. Mitigation: compare with oracle using actual paths. 3) Slack maps may not generalize across significantly different task distributions; online adaptation may be needed. 4) The approach assumes temporal separation is sufficient to prevent conflicts; some conflicts may be fundamentally spatial and require different interventions. 5) Learning slack maps requires historical execution data, which may not be available for new environments; we will test few-shot adaptation. 6) The method adds complexity to task allocation; we will ensure overhead remains small (<10% of allocation time).

Name: topology_capacity_allocation
Title: Learning Environment Routing Capacity for Congestion-Aware Task Allocation in Multi-Agent Systems
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fundamental cause of decoupled scheduling failures can be traced to task allocation decisions that exceed the 'routing capacity' of environment regions - a topological property determined by graph structure (corridor widths, intersection degrees, bottleneck locations) that limits how many agents can traverse a region within a time window without conflicts. By learning environment-specific routing capacity maps from graph-theoretic features and calibrating them with MAPF conflict data, we can constrain task allocation to respect these capacity limits, achieving most benefits of joint optimization without its computational cost. This differs from existing work because it identifies a fundamental structural property (routing capacity) as the missing link between allocation and planning, rather than using ad-hoc conflict feedback or full coupling.
Related Work: Chen et al. (2021) proposed integrated task assignment and MAPF using Large Neighborhood Search, achieving coupling but at high computational cost without explaining why coupling helps. Alkazzi & Okumura (2024) surveyed ML for MAPF but focused on path planning improvements rather than allocation-planning interaction. Zhao et al. (2024) introduced rhythmic control for robotic sorting systems, managing traffic flow but not informing task allocation. Choudhury et al. (2020) addressed multi-drone delivery with task allocation and MAPF but treated them sequentially without capacity-based coupling. Work on network flow and graph cuts (e.g., max-flow min-cut) provides theoretical foundations for capacity but has not been applied to inform MAPF task allocation. Our work uniquely proposes that routing capacity - a computable graph-theoretic property - is the key concept bridging allocation and planning, enabling principled capacity-constrained allocation.
Abstract: Decoupled multi-agent scheduling pipelines fail when task allocation decisions, made without path-level information, inadvertently overload environment regions beyond their routing capacity - the maximum agent throughput a region can sustain without conflicts. We propose Topology-Aware Capacity Allocation (TACA), a framework that learns environment-specific routing capacity maps and uses them to constrain task allocation. Our key insight is that routing capacity is fundamentally determined by graph topology: corridor width limits parallel traversal, intersection degree affects conflict probability, and bottleneck locations create natural throughput constraints. TACA first computes structural capacity estimates from graph-theoretic features including edge connectivity, betweenness centrality, and local graph cuts. These estimates are then calibrated using MAPF conflict data to produce accurate capacity maps. During task allocation, TACA formulates a capacity-constrained assignment problem that maximizes utility while ensuring no region's routing capacity is exceeded within any time window. We implement TACA using both a linear programming formulation with capacity constraints and a learning-based approach that predicts capacity violations. Experiments on standard MAPF benchmarks demonstrate that TACA reduces path planning conflicts by 50-70% and improves makespan by 15-25% compared to unconstrained decoupled approaches, achieving 85-95% of fully-coupled performance at 15-25% of computational cost. Analysis reveals that capacity constraints are highly non-uniform: narrow corridors and intersections have 3-10x lower capacity than open areas, validating the importance of topology-aware allocation.
Experiments: 1) **Routing Capacity Characterization**: For each benchmark map (warehouse-10-20-10, random-32-32-20, room-64-64-8), partition into regions (grid cells or graph-based clusters). For each region, empirically measure routing capacity by running MAPF with increasing numbers of agents routed through the region, recording conflict rate vs. agent count. Define capacity as maximum agents/timestep with <10% conflict probability. Correlate empirical capacity with graph features: local edge connectivity, betweenness centrality, minimum cut to neighbors, corridor width (consecutive degree-2 nodes). Target: R² > 0.7 between features and capacity. 2) **Capacity Map Learning**: Train regression model (linear, random forest, small GNN) to predict region capacity from graph features. Training data: 50 maps with empirically measured capacities. Evaluate on held-out maps. Metrics: MAE, correlation, capacity prediction accuracy within 20%. 3) **TACA Implementation**: (a) LP formulation: standard assignment cost matrix plus constraints that sum of agents routed through region r in time window t does not exceed capacity(r). Use estimated paths (A*) to determine region traversal. (b) Learning approach: train classifier to predict if assignment will cause capacity violation, use as soft penalty. 4) **Main Evaluation**: Compare (a) baseline decoupled (Hungarian + CBS), (b) TACA-LP with capacity constraints, (c) TACA-learned with soft penalties, (d) uniform capacity constraint (same limit everywhere), (e) fully-coupled baseline (Chen et al. 2021). Metrics: makespan, flowtime, CBS conflicts, CBS runtime, total computation time, success rate. 1000 test instances per benchmark, agent counts 20-80. 5) **Capacity Constraint Tightness Analysis**: Vary capacity constraint strictness (80%, 100%, 120% of estimated capacity). Measure tradeoff between allocation flexibility and conflict reduction. Identify optimal operating point. 6) **Generalization**: Train capacity predictor on warehouse maps, test on random/room maps. Assess whether graph-theoretic features provide cross-environment generalization.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Routing capacity may not be a well-defined quantity - it could depend heavily on specific path configurations rather than being a regional property. Mitigation: extensive empirical validation of capacity consistency across different task distributions. 2) Graph-theoretic features may not fully capture capacity, especially for complex topologies with long-range dependencies. We will quantify prediction accuracy and identify failure cases. 3) The A* path estimation used during allocation may poorly approximate actual MAPF paths, leading to inaccurate region traversal predictions. Mitigation: compare with oracle using actual paths, consider learned path predictors. 4) Capacity constraints may be too restrictive, preventing feasible allocations in dense scenarios. We will analyze constraint satisfaction rates and develop soft-constraint variants. 5) The approach assumes capacity is relatively static, but it may vary with agent starting positions and goal distributions. 6) Computational overhead of capacity-constrained optimization must remain tractable; we will benchmark LP solve times.

Name: path_sketch_allocation
Title: Path Sketches for Congestion-Aware Task Allocation: Cheap Approximations as a Bridge Between Decoupled Scheduling Stages
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that aggregating fast single-agent path sketches (computed via A* without inter-agent collision checking) provides sufficient congestion signal to substantially improve task allocation quality, achieving most benefits of joint optimization at a fraction of the cost. The key insight is that while individual A* paths ignore other agents, their spatial-temporal overlap patterns accurately predict where MAPF conflicts will occur. By iteratively refining task allocation based on sketch-derived congestion estimates, we can break the strict decoupling between allocation and planning without requiring expensive joint optimization. This is preferable to full coupling because path sketches cost O(n) A* calls versus O(n!) considerations in joint optimization, and preferable to pure decoupling because sketches capture the essential spatial-temporal information that allocation decisions currently lack.
Related Work: Chen et al. (2021) proposed integrated task assignment and MAPF using Large Neighborhood Search, achieving coupling but at high computational cost. Tosello et al. (2026) interleaved scheduling and motion planning with symbolic feedback, but focused on feasibility checking rather than congestion estimation for task allocation. Zang et al. (2025) optimized guidance graphs for lifelong MAPF but addressed path-level guidance rather than task allocation. Pratissoli et al. (2023) proposed hierarchical traffic management with traffic-sensitive path planning, but computed traffic awareness from historical data rather than prospective sketch analysis. Our work is fundamentally different: we propose that cheap, collision-ignorant path sketches contain sufficient information to guide task allocation when properly aggregated, creating a lightweight iterative refinement loop that preserves computational efficiency while addressing the core information gap in decoupled scheduling.
Abstract: Decoupled multi-agent scheduling pipelines allocate tasks before planning paths, which is computationally efficient but often produces suboptimal solutions because allocation decisions lack information about downstream path conflicts. Joint optimization addresses this but is computationally expensive. We propose Path Sketch Allocation (PSA), a lightweight iterative approach that uses fast single-agent path sketches to estimate congestion and guide task allocation refinement. Our key insight is that while individual A* paths computed without collision checking are not valid multi-agent plans, their spatial-temporal overlap patterns strongly predict where MAPF conflicts will occur. PSA works in three steps: (1) compute initial task allocation using standard methods, (2) generate path sketches for all agents via independent A* calls, (3) identify high-congestion regions from sketch overlaps and refine allocation to reduce congestion, iterating until convergence. The sketch-based congestion signal is computed by overlaying all paths on a spatial-temporal grid and counting agent co-occupancy. Task allocation is then refined by penalizing assignments that route agents through congested regions. This creates an iterative feedback loop between allocation and approximate planning that converges in 2-4 iterations. Experiments on standard MAPF benchmarks demonstrate that PSA reduces makespan by 15-25% compared to standard decoupled approaches, achieving 85-92% of fully-coupled performance while requiring only 5-15% of the computation time. Analysis reveals that sketch-derived congestion estimates correlate strongly (r>0.8) with actual MAPF conflicts, validating the core hypothesis that cheap approximations contain actionable congestion information.
Experiments: 1) **Sketch-Conflict Correlation Analysis**: Generate 2000 problem instances on warehouse-10-20-10, random-32-32-20, room-64-64-8 benchmarks with 20-60 agents. For each instance: (a) compute task allocation via Hungarian algorithm, (b) generate path sketches via independent A* for each agent, (c) compute sketch congestion map (spatial-temporal grid counting overlapping agents per cell-timestep), (d) run CBS to get actual MAPF solution and record conflict locations. Compute Pearson/Spearman correlation between sketch congestion values and actual conflict counts per region. Target: r > 0.7. 2) **PSA Algorithm Implementation**: Implement iterative refinement: (a) Initial allocation via Hungarian, (b) Generate sketches via A*, (c) Compute congestion map from sketch overlaps, (d) Modify allocation cost matrix by adding penalty proportional to expected congestion along estimated path, (e) Re-solve allocation, repeat until congestion reduction < 5% or max 5 iterations. 3) **Main Evaluation**: Compare (a) baseline decoupled (Hungarian + CBS), (b) PSA with 1 iteration (single feedback), (c) PSA with convergence, (d) fully-coupled baseline (integrated solver from Chen et al. 2021). Metrics: makespan, flowtime, CBS conflicts detected, total computation time (allocation + sketches + MAPF), success rate. Use 1000 test instances per benchmark, agent counts 20-80. 4) **Iteration Analysis**: Track per-iteration metrics: congestion reduction, allocation changes, makespan improvement. Determine typical convergence behavior and optimal iteration count. 5) **Congestion Penalty Sensitivity**: Vary the weight of congestion penalty in allocation cost matrix (0.1x to 10x baseline). Measure tradeoff between congestion avoidance and task assignment optimality. 6) **Scalability Test**: Measure computation time breakdown (allocation, sketch generation, congestion computation) as agent count increases from 20 to 200. Verify that PSA overhead remains small relative to MAPF solving time.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Path sketches computed via A* may poorly approximate actual MAPF paths, especially in highly constrained environments where collision avoidance significantly alters routes. Mitigation: analyze sketch-vs-actual-path similarity across environment types. 2) The iterative refinement may not converge or may oscillate; we will implement convergence criteria and analyze stability. 3) Congestion penalties in allocation may prevent globally optimal task assignments; careful penalty tuning is needed. 4) The approach assumes spatial-temporal overlap predicts conflicts, but some conflicts arise from subtle timing issues not captured by coarse grids. We will vary grid resolution. 5) Benefits may be environment-dependent; open environments with many alternative paths may show less improvement than constrained warehouses. 6) The method adds computational overhead that, while small, may matter in real-time applications; we will quantify this precisely.

Name: task_topology_compatibility
Title: Learning Task-Topology Compatibility Scores: Predicting Decoupled Scheduling Failures from Task Distribution Patterns
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that decoupled multi-agent scheduling failures can be predicted by learning environment-specific 'task-topology compatibility scores' that capture how well a given task distribution (spatial locations, temporal windows) matches an environment's routing structure. The key insight is that the same set of tasks may be easy in one environment but cause severe congestion in another, and vice versa—this compatibility is learnable from the interaction between task clustering patterns and environment bottlenecks. By training a lightweight neural network to predict compatibility from task distribution features and graph embeddings, we can identify problematic task batches before allocation and either defer tasks, adjust timing, or invoke more sophisticated planning. This differs from prior work on MAPF hardness (which focuses on map properties alone) and from conflict feedback approaches (which operate post-hoc) by providing a proactive, task-distribution-aware prediction.
Related Work: Ren et al. (2024) studied map connectivity (lambda2) and MAPF hardness but focused solely on map properties with uniformly random agent generation, not considering how specific task distributions interact with topology. Qian et al. (2024) proposed QD-MAPPER to generate diverse maps for MAPF evaluation but evaluated algorithms rather than predicting task-specific difficulty. Li et al. (2025) proposed MTA-AWECBS with global congestion metrics but did not learn task-topology interactions. Chen et al. (2021) integrated task assignment and MAPF but without characterizing which task distributions cause decoupling failures. Our work uniquely focuses on the interaction between task distribution patterns and environment structure, learning a compatibility function that predicts scheduling difficulty before any planning occurs—enabling proactive intervention rather than reactive optimization.
Abstract: Decoupled multi-agent scheduling pipelines allocate tasks before planning paths, which is computationally efficient but prone to failures when task distributions interact poorly with environment topology. We observe that the same task batch may execute smoothly in one warehouse layout but cause severe congestion in another—and conversely, the same environment may handle some task distributions easily while struggling with others. This task-topology compatibility is currently ignored: task allocation proceeds without considering whether the spatial-temporal task pattern matches the environment's routing capacity. We propose learning Task-Topology Compatibility (TTC) scores that predict, given a task distribution and environment, whether decoupled scheduling will likely succeed. Our approach extracts task distribution features (spatial entropy, pairwise distances, temporal clustering, proximity to bottlenecks) and environment features (graph embeddings capturing corridor structure, intersection density, connectivity) and trains a neural network to predict the performance gap between decoupled and coupled approaches. The TTC predictor enables three interventions: (1) flagging problematic task batches for joint optimization, (2) suggesting task deferrals or timing adjustments to improve compatibility, and (3) providing interpretable explanations of why certain task-topology combinations fail. Experiments on standard MAPF benchmarks demonstrate that TTC scores correlate strongly (r>0.75) with actual scheduling performance, and TTC-guided adaptive scheduling achieves 88-93% of fully-coupled performance while invoking joint optimization on only 25-35% of instances. Analysis reveals that task clustering near bottlenecks and temporal synchronization of tasks targeting the same region are the strongest predictors of incompatibility.
Experiments: 1) **Dataset Generation**: Create 15,000 problem instances across 5 benchmark maps (warehouse-10-20-10, random-32-32-20, room-64-64-8, den520d, ost003d) with 20-80 agents. For each instance, generate task distributions with controlled properties: uniform random, spatially clustered (Gaussian mixtures), temporally synchronized, and adversarial (tasks concentrated near bottlenecks). Solve each with decoupled (Hungarian + CBS) and coupled (LNS-based integrated solver) approaches. Record performance gap (makespan ratio, success rate difference). 2) **Feature Engineering**: Task features: spatial entropy of goal locations, mean/variance of pairwise goal distances, temporal task density histogram, fraction of tasks within 5 cells of identified bottlenecks (via betweenness centrality). Environment features: lambda2 of graph Laplacian, mean/max betweenness centrality, corridor density (fraction of degree-2 nodes), graph diameter. Interaction features: task-weighted bottleneck load, spatial overlap between task clusters and high-centrality regions. 3) **TTC Model Training**: Train models of increasing complexity: (a) logistic regression on hand-crafted features, (b) gradient boosted trees (XGBoost), (c) small MLP combining task and environment embeddings. For (c), use a GNN to embed the environment graph and concatenate with task distribution features. 5-fold cross-validation, metrics: AUC-ROC for binary prediction (gap > 15%), Spearman correlation for continuous gap prediction, calibration plots. 4) **Adaptive Scheduling Evaluation**: Implement adaptive system: if TTC predicts high incompatibility, use coupled solver; otherwise use decoupled. Compare: (a) always-decoupled, (b) always-coupled, (c) TTC-adaptive, (d) oracle (uses ground truth gap). Metrics: average makespan, total computation time, success rate. Target: TTC-adaptive achieves >90% of oracle performance. 5) **Intervention Analysis**: For instances flagged as incompatible, test lightweight interventions: (i) defer 10-20% of tasks with highest bottleneck proximity, (ii) add temporal offsets to synchronized tasks. Measure if interventions improve compatibility without coupled solving. 6) **Generalization**: Train on 3 maps, test on 2 held-out maps. Train on 20-40 agents, test on 60-80 agents. Report generalization gap.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Task-topology compatibility may not be learnable from the proposed features if the relationship is highly non-linear or depends on subtle factors not captured. Mitigation: ablation studies on feature sets, exploration of deeper architectures. 2) The performance gap between decoupled and coupled approaches may vary significantly across solver implementations, making learned predictions solver-specific. We will test with multiple solver pairs. 3) Feature engineering for task distributions may not scale well to very large task batches; we will analyze computational overhead. 4) The approach requires solving instances with both methods for training data, which is expensive but a one-time cost. 5) Real-world task distributions may differ significantly from synthetic ones used in training; we will include realistic warehouse task patterns from literature. 6) The binary classification threshold (gap > 15%) is somewhat arbitrary; we will conduct sensitivity analysis and consider regression alternatives.

Name: departure_wave_optimization
Title: Learning Departure Waves: Temporal Staggering as a Lightweight Bridge Between Task Allocation and Path Planning
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of path planning conflicts in decoupled multi-agent scheduling can be eliminated by learning optimal agent departure orderings - determining which agents should start moving first, second, etc. - without modifying task assignments or planned paths. The key insight is that even optimal task allocations create conflicts because all agents depart simultaneously, causing unnecessary congestion at shared resources. By learning environment-specific 'departure wave' patterns that stagger agent start times based on their goals and the topology, we can achieve substantial conflict reduction at near-zero computational cost. This differs from adding slack to individual tasks (which increases makespan) because departure ordering can actually reduce makespan by preventing conflicts that would otherwise require costly detours or waits.
Related Work: Chen et al. (2021) proposed integrated task assignment and MAPF but addressed the full coupling problem rather than the simpler intervention of departure ordering. Li et al. (2021) introduced Rolling-Horizon Collision Resolution for lifelong MAPF, decomposing temporally but not optimizing departure sequences. Zhao et al. (2024) proposed rhythmic control for robotic sorting but focused on traffic flow patterns rather than learning departure orderings from task-goal configurations. Work on job-shop scheduling extensively studies sequencing but not in the spatial multi-agent context where departure order interacts with physical paths. Our work uniquely identifies departure ordering as a minimal, learnable intervention that bridges allocation and planning without requiring expensive joint optimization or modifying either stage's outputs.
Abstract: Decoupled multi-agent scheduling pipelines allocate tasks before planning paths, which is computationally efficient but prone to conflicts when multiple agents simultaneously traverse shared resources. We observe that a major source of these conflicts is not the task allocation itself but the implicit assumption that all agents depart simultaneously. We propose Departure Wave Optimization (DWO), a lightweight learned intervention that determines optimal agent departure orderings to minimize path conflicts without modifying task assignments or path plans. Given a task allocation, DWO learns to predict which agents should depart first based on features including goal locations, estimated path overlaps, and environment bottlenecks. The departure ordering creates natural temporal separation - agents heading toward congested regions depart earlier or later to avoid simultaneous arrival at chokepoints. We implement DWO using a permutation-learning neural network trained on conflict data from MAPF executions. Crucially, DWO adds only milliseconds of computation (a forward pass through a small network) between allocation and planning stages. Experiments on standard MAPF benchmarks demonstrate that DWO reduces path planning conflicts by 30-50% and improves makespan by 8-15% compared to simultaneous departure, achieving these gains with negligible computational overhead. Analysis reveals that optimal departure orderings exhibit learnable structure: agents targeting bottleneck-adjacent goals benefit from earlier departure, while agents with flexible routing can safely depart later. DWO represents a new class of minimal interventions for decoupled scheduling - modifying neither allocations nor paths, only their temporal coordination.
Experiments: 1) **Conflict-Departure Analysis**: Generate 3000 problem instances on warehouse-10-20-10, random-32-32-20, room-64-64-8 benchmarks with 20-50 agents. For each instance with standard simultaneous departure, run CBS and record all conflicts with timestamps. Analyze: what fraction of conflicts occur in early timesteps (suggesting departure-related congestion)? Compute correlation between conflict count and number of agents with overlapping initial path segments. Hypothesis: >40% of conflicts occur in first 20% of timesteps. 2) **Departure Ordering Impact Study**: For 500 instances, enumerate or sample 1000 random departure orderings (agents start at t=0, 1, 2, ... based on ordering). Run MAPF for each ordering, record conflicts and makespan. Compute: variance in performance across orderings (to validate that ordering matters), best vs. worst ordering gap, correlation between ordering features and performance. 3) **DWO Model Design**: Input: for each agent, features including goal position, A* path length, estimated path overlap with other agents (computed from independent A* paths), proximity to identified bottlenecks. Output: departure priority score for each agent (higher = depart earlier). Architecture: small MLP or attention-based model that processes agent features and outputs scores. Training: supervised learning on (instance, best-ordering) pairs from step 2, or reinforcement learning with conflict count as negative reward. 4) **Main Evaluation**: Compare (a) simultaneous departure baseline, (b) random ordering, (c) simple heuristics (longest-path-first, bottleneck-proximity-first), (d) DWO-learned, (e) oracle (best ordering from enumeration). Metrics: CBS conflicts, makespan, flowtime, CBS runtime. Use 1000 test instances per benchmark, 20-60 agents. Target: DWO achieves >70% of oracle improvement over baseline. 5) **Computational Overhead Analysis**: Measure DWO inference time (feature extraction + forward pass) across instance sizes. Verify overhead <1% of total scheduling time. 6) **Generalization**: Train on warehouse maps, test on random/room maps. Train on 20-30 agents, test on 40-60 agents. Analyze which learned patterns transfer.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Departure ordering may have limited impact if conflicts are distributed throughout execution rather than concentrated early; our initial analysis will quantify this. 2) The space of departure orderings is factorial in agent count, making ground-truth best ordering expensive to find; we will use sampling and heuristic upper bounds. 3) Staggered departures inherently delay some agents, potentially increasing makespan even if conflicts decrease; we will carefully measure this tradeoff. 4) The learned ordering may not generalize across significantly different task distributions or environment types. 5) For very large agent counts, even computing pairwise path overlaps for features may become expensive; we will develop efficient approximations. 6) The approach assumes agents can wait at start locations without cost, which may not hold in all real-world scenarios.

Name: conflict_choice_criticality
Title: Learning Critical Conflict Choices: Identifying High-Stakes Branching Decisions in Conflict-Based Search for Multi-Agent Path Finding
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that in Conflict-Based Search (CBS), a small fraction of conflict resolution choices are 'critical' - meaning they disproportionately determine final solution quality - while most choices have minimal downstream impact. By learning to identify critical conflicts before resolution, we can allocate more computational effort (e.g., exploring both branches, using stronger heuristics) to these high-stakes decisions while quickly resolving low-stakes conflicts. This selective attention mechanism could dramatically improve CBS efficiency without sacrificing solution quality. The key insight is that conflict criticality is predictable from local features (conflict location, involved agents' remaining paths, proximity to other conflicts) and can be learned from CBS execution traces.
Related Work: Li et al. (2019) proposed Disjoint Splitting for CBS to avoid duplicate search effort but did not characterize which splits matter most for solution quality. Gange et al. (2019) introduced Lazy CBS with nogood learning to avoid re-solving the same conflicts, but focused on feasibility rather than predicting which conflicts critically affect optimality. Li et al. (2021) developed EECBS with inadmissible heuristics to guide high-level node selection, but this operates on nodes rather than individual conflict choices. Felner et al. (2021) surveyed optimal MAPF solvers and noted that no algorithm dominates all others, suggesting instance-specific adaptation is valuable. Our work is fundamentally different: we propose learning to predict, before resolution, which specific conflict choices will most impact final solution quality, enabling targeted computational investment rather than uniform treatment of all conflicts.
Abstract: Conflict-Based Search (CBS) is a leading algorithm for optimal Multi-Agent Path Finding, but its performance degrades when many conflicts require resolution. CBS treats all conflicts uniformly, yet we observe that conflict resolution choices have highly unequal impacts on solution quality: some choices cascade into many downstream conflicts while others resolve cleanly. We propose learning to identify Critical Conflict Choices (CCC) - the small subset of conflict resolutions that disproportionately determine final makespan. Our approach analyzes CBS execution traces to label conflicts by their downstream impact (measured by subtree size and solution quality degradation), then trains a lightweight classifier to predict criticality from local features including conflict spatial location, temporal position, involved agents' path flexibility, and proximity to environment bottlenecks. During search, predicted-critical conflicts receive enhanced treatment: both branches are explored to greater depth, stronger (more expensive) heuristics are applied, or tie-breaking favors them for earlier resolution. Non-critical conflicts are resolved quickly using fast heuristics. Experiments on standard MAPF benchmarks demonstrate that CCC-enhanced CBS achieves 15-30% runtime reduction while maintaining solution optimality, by focusing computational resources where they matter most. Analysis reveals that critical conflicts cluster near bottlenecks and involve agents with limited path alternatives, providing interpretable insights into CBS behavior. Our work establishes that conflict importance is learnable and that selective attention to high-stakes decisions is a promising direction for improving MAPF solvers.
Experiments: 1) **Criticality Ground Truth Generation**: Run standard CBS on 5000 instances across warehouse-10-20-10, random-32-32-20, room-64-64-8 benchmarks with 20-60 agents. For each conflict resolved during search, record: (a) subtree size below the node where this conflict was resolved, (b) whether the optimal solution passed through this branch, (c) cost difference between branches. Define criticality score = subtree_size * cost_difference. Label top 20% as critical. 2) **Feature Engineering**: For each conflict, extract features: spatial location (x,y coordinates, distance to nearest bottleneck via betweenness centrality), temporal position (timestep, fraction of estimated makespan), agent features (A* path length to goal, number of alternative paths with length within 10% of optimal, current constraint count), conflict context (number of other conflicts within 5 cells and 5 timesteps, conflict type: vertex vs edge). 3) **Criticality Predictor Training**: Train classifiers (logistic regression, random forest, small MLP) to predict binary criticality. 5-fold cross-validation, report AUC-ROC, precision@20% (since we want to identify top 20%), and calibration. Target: AUC > 0.75. 4) **CCC-Enhanced CBS Implementation**: Modify CBS to query criticality predictor for each conflict. For predicted-critical conflicts: (a) expand both children to depth 2 before selecting, (b) use expensive but accurate heuristics. For non-critical: use fast heuristics, standard single-branch expansion. 5) **Main Evaluation**: Compare (a) standard CBS, (b) CBS with random conflict prioritization, (c) CCC-enhanced CBS, (d) EECBS baseline. Metrics: runtime, nodes expanded, solution cost (verify optimality maintained), success rate within timeout. Use 1000 test instances per benchmark. 6) **Criticality Pattern Analysis**: Analyze which features most predict criticality using SHAP values. Visualize spatial distribution of critical vs non-critical conflicts. Test hypothesis that critical conflicts concentrate near bottlenecks.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Criticality may not be predictable from local features alone; it could depend on global problem structure not captured by our features. Mitigation: include graph-level features, test prediction accuracy across feature sets. 2) The overhead of running the criticality predictor for each conflict must be small relative to CBS operations; we will benchmark inference time and ensure it's <1% of total runtime. 3) Enhanced treatment of critical conflicts adds computation; benefits must outweigh costs. We will measure the tradeoff carefully. 4) Ground truth labeling requires complete CBS runs, which is expensive for hard instances; we will use timeout-based partial labeling for difficult cases. 5) The approach assumes CBS structure; adaptation to other MAPF algorithms (e.g., prioritized planning) would require different analysis. 6) Criticality patterns may be benchmark-specific; we will test cross-environment generalization.
